turns.content: I'm stuck on this weird memory corruption issue when my Go binary calls into our library. I tried adding link attribute to the FFI module but it's still segfaulting at the boundary.
That usually means your C struct layout isn't matching what the C side expects. Have you checked if every #[repr(C)] on the Rust structs is actually being respected by the linker?
I have #[repr(C)] on all of them, but I suspect the padding might be off. When I print the memory dump from both sides it looks different even though the fields are in the same order.
The difference is almost certainly in how the compiler handles alignment for C vs Rust types. You need to explicitly set #[repr(C)] on every field inside the struct, not just the root struct itself.
Oh right, I only put it at the top level. So if I have a nested struct with an array or a pointer, those internal fields might be misaligned too?
Exactly. For arrays and pointers inside FFI structs you must declare #[repr(C)] on each inner type as well otherwise the binary layout diverges.
That makes sense, but what about the linking attribute I was trying to use? Is link attribute enough or do I also need cbindgen?

link attribute tells the linker how to treat the library but it does not fix layout mismatches. Use cbindgen to generate the header so you can verify the exact byte offsets match what your C code expects.
I ran cbindgen and the generated header looks perfect, yet my Go side is still reading garbage. Did I miss something in the extern block?
Check that your extern "C" function signatures match exactly what C expects including return types and argument names. Sometimes a missing const or wrong type causes silent misalignment.
Wait, I just realized my Rust function returns &mut [u8] but the Go side is passing by value. That means the pointer itself isn't being treated as a C-compatible opaque handle.

That's the core issue. In FFI you cannot return mutable references directly to C because they aren't stable across the boundary. You need to wrap your data in a #[repr(C)] struct that holds only raw pointers or owned slices.
So I should define a wrapper type with link attribute and pass that instead of the slice directly? And make sure all internal fields are marked repr(C)?
Yes, exactly. Create an opaque FFI struct with #[repr(C)] and #[link = "c"] then return a pointer to that wrapper from your extern function.

I'm stuck on a weird edge case in our new project. I have a struct defined with #[repr(C)] and it gets passed through an extern "C" block, but when linking the shared library, the symbol is missing even though I think everything looks right. My code uses link attribute to specify the target crate for the FFI boundary, yet the linker complains about undefined references.

That sounds like a classic mismatch between your Rust definitions and how the C side is expecting them. When you use #[repr(C)] on a struct, you must ensure every field has an explicit type and size that matches exactly what the C code expects. If your Rust struct has any hidden padding or alignment differences, the linker will fail because the binary layout won't match the C ABI. You should verify that all fields in your extern "C" block are primitive types like i32, f64, or pointers without any nested complex types unless they are also marked #[repr(C)].

I checked the struct and it looks fine. The fields are all simple. But here's the thing - I'm using bindgen to generate the header, and cbindgen is telling me that my Rust type doesn't match the C definition. It says there's a mismatch in field alignment or order. Can you help me understand what cbindgen actually checks for?

cbindgen specifically validates that your Rust struct layout exactly matches the declared C header structure it generates. It checks both the ordering of fields and their alignment attributes. If you define a struct in Rust without #[repr(C)], cbindgen will assume default Rust packing rules which may differ from standard C ABI requirements. For FFI safety, every struct exposed to C must be explicitly annotated with #[repr(C)] so that memory layout is guaranteed to match the C header regardless of platform-specific alignment rules.

Okay, I added #[repr(C)] and cbindgen is happy now. But then I hit another issue - when I try to call a function from Rust into a C library using extern "C", the symbol resolution fails even though the header looks correct. It seems like the linker can't find the actual function definition in the shared object file.

This often happens when you're not properly specifying which crate or library contains the C symbols. The link attribute on your extern "C" block tells Rust where to look for those definitions, but if that path doesn't match exactly what the linker expects, it will fail silently or produce warnings. You need to ensure the link attribute points directly to the shared object file being built, not just a target crate name. Sometimes you also need to add explicit dependency declarations so the build system knows to include the right libraries during linking.

I tried adding more dependencies but it still doesn't work. Is there something about how extern "C" blocks interact with cargo's link phase that I'm missing? Like, does cargo automatically handle the symbol export?

Cargo does handle symbol exports for FFI, but it relies on proper configuration in your build script. When you define extern "C" blocks, Cargo expects them to be properly annotated with link attribute so it knows which crate the symbols belong to. If you're building a shared library manually with cargo, make sure you're using --lib or --bin flags appropriately and that your C code is compiled into the same artifact. Sometimes adding explicit linker arguments in build.rs helps resolve symbol visibility issues.

I just realized something - I was compiling my Rust code as a static lib instead of a shared one. That might be why the symbols aren't being exported properly. But even with --shared, I'm getting undefined reference errors for some functions.

That's exactly right - static libraries don't export symbols by default in most contexts unless you explicitly tell the linker to do so. When using #[cfg_attr(target_os = "linux", link = "..."), you're telling Rust which shared object contains your C functions. If that file doesn't exist or isn't being linked, cargo will fail with undefined references. You should verify that all your extern "C" blocks have valid link attributes pointing to actual existing shared libraries.

I found the issue - one of my functions was declared without a link attribute even though it's part of an FFI boundary. But there's another gotcha I noticed - when I pass pointers through extern "C" blocks, they seem to get converted incorrectly.

Pointers in Rust are always sized by word size on most platforms, but C uses pointer sizes directly. When you declare a function parameter as *mut i32 in extern "C" block, it gets wrapped into a concrete type like *const i32 or *mut i32 depending on your Rust version and ABI rules. You need to ensure that all pointer types are explicitly marked with #[repr(C)] if they're part of complex struct layouts, otherwise the memory layout might not match what C expects.
I think I understand now - the key is consistency across all FFI boundaries. But I'm still confused about how cbindgen handles pointer types versus regular values. Does it treat them differently?

cbindgen treats pointers specially because they represent memory addresses, not actual data values. When you generate headers with cbindgen, it will output the correct C-style pointer declarations like const char* or int*, but only if your Rust structs are properly annotated with #[repr(C)]. If you have a struct containing a pointer field without #[repr(C)], cbindgen might generate incorrect type signatures that won't compile in C.

Okay, I've fixed the struct definitions and added proper link attributes. But now I'm trying to test my FFI code with a simple C program, and it's crashing immediately upon calling into Rust. The stack trace shows some weird memory access issues.

This sounds like an alignment or padding issue again. When you pass structs through FFI boundaries, even small differences in field ordering can cause the wrong data to be read due to misaligned offsets. Check that your Rust struct matches the C header byte-for-byte. You might also want to use a helper function from std::mem::size_of or std::ptr::read_unaligned to safely extract values without assuming alignment.
I added some safety checks but it's still unstable. What about using traits like Send or Sync on my FFI types? Does that help with linking?

Traits like Send and Sync are useful for thread safety, not for symbol resolution during linking. They don't affect how the linker finds your symbols. For FFI specifically, focus on ensuring your #[repr(C)] annotations match exactly with C headers, proper link attributes in extern "C" blocks, and correct pointer handling. Traits won't solve alignment or symbol visibility problems.
I think I need to rebuild everything from scratch then. But before I do that, can you clarify the difference between link attribute on extern "C" vs regular functions?

The link attribute is specifically for FFI boundaries where Rust needs to locate symbols in external libraries. Regular functions don't need this because they're compiled into your own crate. For extern "C" blocks, the link attribute tells cargo which shared library contains the C implementation of those functions. Without it, Rust won know how to resolve the symbols when building a shared object.
Perfect, that clears things up. One last question - what's the best practice for documenting these FFI boundaries so other developers can understand them later?

The best approach is to use cbindgen consistently throughout your project and keep the generated headers in a dedicated directory. Document any manual overrides or special cases in comments within your Rust code next to the extern "C" blocks. This way, developers can see exactly what C header was expected and why certain #[repr(C)] annotations were added.

Thanks for walking me through this. I feel much more confident about handling FFI boundaries now. Just to confirm - the three key things are: consistent #[repr(C)], proper link attributes, and using cbindgen to keep headers in sync.
Exactly those three elements are crucial for reliable FFI. Once you get those right, most symbol resolution and alignment issues will resolve themselves. Good luck with your project!

I just managed to get my C library calling into a simple binary using an extern block, but I'm getting weird linkage errors. It seems like the compiler isn't treating my structs correctly when they cross boundaries.

Those linker symbols are definitely being treated as local to your crate instead of exported for your C side. You need to wrap those Rust types in #[repr(C)] so the memory layout matches what C expects, otherwise the ABI won't align when you call back into the C library.
I added that attribute but now I'm getting a symbol not found error. It looks like my Rust wrapper isn't being linked properly against the shared object file at runtime.

You need to tell the compiler explicitly which crate you want to link against so it knows where those symbols live. Use the link attribute on your extern block to point directly to that shared library, otherwise the linker can't find the implementation at runtime.

Okay I added the link attribute but now I'm wondering if I should be generating headers myself or using a tool. It seems like my manual struct definitions are getting messy and inconsistent with the C side.

Using cbindgen is definitely the way to go here since it will automatically generate header files that match your Rust types exactly, so you won't have to manually maintain those definitions anymore. It reads your #[repr(C)] structs and outputs a clean C header for your FFI.
That sounds good but I'm worried about the complexity. Can cbindgen handle nested structures or just flat ones? My C library has some complex data types with pointers inside them.

cbindgen can definitely handle nested structures and complex layouts, but you need to make sure your Rust structs are marked #[repr(C)] so the memory layout matches what C expects. If there are arrays or pointers involved, cbindgen will generate the correct declarations for those too.
I'm still getting some weird alignment issues with my structs when I try to access them from C. It seems like the padding is wrong because Rust is optimizing differently than C.

That's exactly why #[repr(C)] is critical here - it forces the compiler to ignore its own alignment optimizations and match the C ABI exactly, so there won't be any unexpected padding between fields. Without that attribute, Rust might add alignment bytes that break your C code.
I understand now but what about when I call functions from C into Rust? Do I need to do anything special there or is the extern block enough?

The extern block with #[repr(C)] on your arguments and return types should be sufficient for most cases, but you might want to check that your function signatures match exactly between C and Rust. The link attribute ensures the symbols are exported correctly so both sides can call each other.
I'm getting errors about undefined reference when I try to build my Rust library now. It seems like the linker isn't finding my C functions that I'm calling from Rust code.

You need to make sure your Cargo.toml has the correct dependencies listed so the build system knows which libraries to link against when you compile. If you're using cbindgen, it should generate the necessary configuration files for linking automatically.
I checked the config but still getting errors. It seems like my extern blocks aren't being recognized as part of the public API that needs to be exported.

You might need to use #[doc(hidden)] or ensure your functions are actually marked as pub so they get included in the crate's symbol table. The link attribute combined with proper visibility attributes should make sure those symbols are exported correctly.
I think I finally got it working but now I'm wondering about performance implications of all these extra steps. Is there any overhead from the FFI compared to calling functions directly?

There is a slight overhead due to the context switch between processes and memory marshaling, but for most applications this is negligible. The main performance concern would be if you're doing extremely tight loops with millions of cross-language calls.
I'm still not entirely sure about the exact syntax for my extern blocks though. Can you give me a concrete example of how to structure this properly?

You'll need an extern block with #[link(...)] pointing to your C library, then define functions with #[repr(C)] on their arguments and return values so the memory layout matches what C expects. This ensures both sides can interoperate correctly without ABI mismatches.

I'm debugging a crash where my Rust code is calling into C, and I thought the problem was just that I didn't export the right functions with link attribute. Can you tell me how to use extern "C" blocks properly so this won't happen again?

The issue isn't just about exporting functions; it's also about how your Rust structs match the C ABI exactly. When defining a struct for FFI, you must use #[repr(C)] to ensure memory layout is identical across both languages. Without this attribute, the compiler might reorder fields or add padding based on Rust alignment rules, causing data corruption when the C side reads it.
I see, so I should just wrap my struct in extern "C"? But what about complex types like Option<T> or Result? Do those even work with FFI?

Complex Rust types like Option or Result cannot be used directly because they don't have a stable memory representation. For FFI, you must define your own C-compatible structs that mirror the data layout of these Rust types manually using #[repr(C)] and explicit field ordering.
That sounds tedious. I just want to pass a simple struct through an FFI boundary without writing new definitions every time. Is there a way to reuse existing Rust structs automatically?

There is no automatic conversion for arbitrary Rust types into C ABI formats. You must manually create the #[repr(C)] wrapper struct and implement its fields as raw pointers or primitive types that match C expectations.
Okay, I understand the manual effort now. But what about generating headers automatically? I keep seeing people mention cbindgen for header generation—does that solve my problem?

cbindgen can generate C headers from Rust code if all your types are marked #[repr(C)] and follow strict naming conventions. However, it only works well when you've already done the manual setup correctly; it doesn't magically fix incorrect struct definitions.

I tried running cbindgen on my project but it complained about missing attributes and I couldn't figure out what to change. It seems like just having extern "C" is not enough for the tool to recognize my types.

cbindgen requires every struct intended for FFI to be annotated with #[repr(C)] and often needs explicit visibility attributes. If your Rust structs lack these annotations, cbindgen will skip them or report errors because it cannot determine their memory layout.
So I need to add #[repr(C)] everywhere? Does that affect performance or stability of my Rust code when compiling?

Adding #[repr(C)] does not impact runtime performance since it only affects compile-time layout. However, it can cause warnings if your struct fields have different sizes than expected by C compilers unless you carefully match alignment and padding.
I'm worried about linking errors now because my generated header doesn't match what the C library expects. How do I verify that the extern "C" interface is actually compatible with the C side?

You can use cargo build --release to compile both sides together and check for undefined symbol errors, then cross-compile from the Rust side using rustc with --crate-type=libdylib if needed. Also ensure your C code uses extern "C" on function calls that return Rust-compatible types.
I still can't get this to work despite adding the attributes and using cbindgen. Is there a pattern I'm missing for handling pointer arguments between C and Rust in extern "C" blocks?

The key is ensuring that pointers are passed as raw pointers (e.g., *mut u8) rather than smart pointers like Box or Rc, since those don't have stable memory layouts. Also, avoid using Vec<T> directly; instead use a flat array with length fields if possible.
Got it. I'll restructure my data structures to avoid smart pointers and ensure all FFI types are #[repr(C)]. But what about error handling? How do I propagate errors from C back to Rust safely?

For error propagation, you can return a special error code enum defined with #[repr(C)] and use it alongside your data structures. Alternatively, you could use a custom exception mechanism or signal-based errors if the C side supports them.
Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to call a legacy C library from my Rust project and the compiler is screaming at me about memory layout mismatches. It feels like magic that just happens.

That frustration is common when bridging systems with different abstractions. The core issue is that C doesn't enforce any specific memory layout for structs, so the Rust compiler assumes padding fields might exist based on its own alignment rules. When you declare extern "C" blocks without #[repr(C)], the Rust struct could have extra bytes of padding at the end or beginning, causing the C side to read garbage data because it expects a compact representation. The solution is always to annotate your structs with #[repr(C)] so the compiler guarantees zero-padding and matches the C memory layout exactly.
Okay so I just add #repr(c) to my struct? But what about linking attributes? How do those relate to this?

Linking attributes are a separate concern from the data layout itself. When you use extern "C" functions, Rust automatically generates an FFI boundary marker in the object file so the dynamic linker knows where your symbol starts and ends. However, if you're using older C libraries or have specific versioning requirements, you might need to explicitly declare the link attribute on your crate or function to control how symbols are exported or hidden from other modules. This is mostly about the binary interface contract rather than the memory layout of the data structures themselves.
Got it, so #repr(c) fixes the struct and link attributes handle the symbol visibility? What if I need to generate header files for my C side?

For generating headers, you should use cbindgen. This tool reads your Rust code with #[repr(C)] annotations and generates a C header file that matches your FFI boundary exactly. It will produce extern "C" functions and struct declarations in the output so you can paste them into your legacy C project. Without proper #repr(c) markers, cbindgen might generate incorrect padding or field orderings that would break compilation on the C side.
That makes sense. But what if my Rust struct has optional fields that don't exist in the C version? How do I handle that?

You have two main options here. First, you can use #[allow(dead_code)] on the unused fields and let cbindgen generate them anyway with a comment noting they're Rust-specific. Second, you might need to create separate structs for C and Rust if the optional nature differs significantly between languages. The key is ensuring that any field present in both sides has matching memory offsets so the pointer arithmetic remains consistent across the boundary.
What about error handling? If I call a C function and it fails, how do I propagate that back to Rust cleanly?

You can't really use Rust's native error types directly across the FFI boundary because they won't have compatible memory layouts. The standard pattern is to define a C-compatible error enum or integer code that represents success and various failure conditions in your header file generated by cbindgen. Then you return these codes from your extern "C" functions, and Rust converts them back into proper Result<T, E> types using match expressions after the call returns.
So I avoid using std::panic! or any Rust-specific error handling inside the FFI function itself?

Exactly. Never let panic or other non-deterministic Rust runtime behavior cross the boundary since it would crash the C process instead of returning a clean exit code. The FFI functions must be pure from Rust's perspective regarding control flow - they only do input validation, call into C libraries safely, and return pre-defined error codes that match your C-side expectations.

I'm looking at a colleague's code where they're trying to call a legacy C function from our new Rust library, but I noticed the struct definition looks weird and the binary size is huge. Can you explain why we need #[repr(C)] on structs when calling C functions?

When you call C code from Rust, the memory layout of your Rust structures must match exactly what the C compiler expects, otherwise the pointers and offsets will be wrong. The #[repr(C)] attribute tells the Rust compiler to enforce a specific memory layout that matches the C standard, ensuring fields are ordered left-to-right as they appear in the source code without padding bytes for alignment that might break the ABI. Without this, the Rust compiler adds padding based on native architecture rules like word size, which means your C side won't see the same data at those offsets it expects.
Okay so if I don't use #[repr(C)], the pointers will just be garbage? Or does it only affect specific platforms?

If you omit #[repr(C)] on a struct, Rust may add padding bytes to align fields according to native architecture rules like word size, which means your C side won't see the same data at those offsets it expects. This is critical for correctness and safety because the ABI contract between Rust and C relies on strict memory layout agreement, not just platform behavior. The padding differences can cause crashes or silent data corruption when crossing language boundaries.
Got it, so alignment matters a lot here. But what about the actual function call itself? Do we need any special syntax besides passing the struct by value?

You generally pass structs to C functions by value if they fit in a register or stack frame, but Rust's `extern` blocks require specific handling for safety and performance. When you declare an extern "C" block with #[repr(C)] structs, you're ensuring the calling convention matches what C expects, so the stack layout remains consistent across language boundaries without needing manual pointer arithmetic.
That makes sense, but I'm worried about linking issues. My colleague mentioned something about link attribute and how it affects symbol visibility? Does that matter for FFI?

Yes, the link attribute controls whether symbols are exported to the linker so they can be resolved from other shared libraries or object files. For FFI, you typically want functions to be visible externally so C code can call them at runtime, while private Rust internals should remain hidden using #[link = "hidden"] to reduce binary size and prevent accidental linkage issues in larger systems.
Hmm, I thought cbindgen was just for generating headers. How does it relate to the extern block syntax we're seeing here?

cbindgen automatically generates C header files from your Rust code by analyzing #[repr(C)] structs and functions marked with extern "C" blocks. It produces a consistent interface definition that matches exactly what your Rust compiler expects, eliminating manual boilerplate and ensuring the generated header reflects the actual ABI constraints of your FFI boundary.
So cbindgen reads the #[repr(C)] annotations and the extern block syntax to produce the correct C declaration? That seems like it would catch my alignment mistakes automatically?

cbindgen does parse #[repr(C)] attributes and extern "C" blocks, but it doesn't guarantee perfect portability across all Rust versions or compilers. You still need to manually verify that the generated header matches your C implementation's expectations because cbindgen operates on syntax rather than runtime ABI verification.
Fair point. But what if I want to call a C function that returns a struct? Does the return value get handled differently in Rust?

When calling a C function returning a struct, you typically allocate space for it on the stack or heap and then copy data into your Rust struct using a safe abstraction layer. The extern "C" block ensures the caller knows how to interpret the binary layout of that returned struct according to the #[repr(C)] specification.
I'm still confused about why my colleague's code has such verbose extern blocks. Can't we just use regular functions and let Rust handle it?

Regular Rust functions don't guarantee C compatibility because Rust may add padding or reorder fields for alignment, which breaks the ABI contract. The extern "C" block explicitly signals to the compiler that this function must follow the C calling convention with no extra padding, ensuring strict memory layout agreement across language boundaries.
So if I remove the extern block and just use a normal function, will it compile fine? Or does it break something?

Removing the extern "C" block won't prevent compilation in isolation, but when linking with C code or calling from C, your Rust struct may have unexpected padding that causes pointer mismatches. The compiler will still generate valid Rust code, but the FFI boundary becomes unreliable because the ABI contract is no longer enforced.
Okay so I need to be careful about #[repr(C)] everywhere in my module? Is there a performance cost?

The #[repr(C)] attribute has minimal runtime overhead since it's purely compile-time metadata, but it does constrain the Rust compiler to generate code that matches C expectations, which might prevent some optimizations like aggressive reordering of fields. The trade-off is correctness over maximum speed in FFI scenarios.
One last thing, if I have multiple structs with different field orders but all #[repr(C)], how does cbindgen handle the differences?

cbindgen generates separate declarations for each struct based on their individual field orderings and types. It won't merge them into one interface unless you explicitly use macros or wrappers, so your generated header will reflect the exact structure of each #[repr(C)] type.

I'm preparing for a technical interview and need to understand how to safely call C libraries from Rust. Specifically, I want to know about the extern "C" blocks and how they interact with #[repr(C)] structs.

The extern "C" block is essential because it tells the compiler that the following code should be compiled for the C ABI rather than the default Rust ABI. When you define a struct inside such a block, you must annotate it with #[repr(C)] to ensure its memory layout matches what C expects. Without this annotation, the compiler might apply Rust-specific optimizations like padding or reordering fields, which would break the binary interface.
That helps clarify the layout issue, but I'm confused about the link attribute part of the extern "C" block. Why do we need to specify the linking section and how does that affect symbol visibility?

The link attribute specifies which shared object file contains the implementation of the function you are calling from Rust. For example, using link("libc") tells the compiler to look for the function in the standard C library. If you omit this or use a different target like "crt", the linker might fail to resolve the symbol at runtime because it won't know where to find the implementation.
I see, so if I'm writing my own C wrapper header and exposing it to Rust, do I need cbindgen for that? And what happens if my struct definitions don't match exactly between the two sides?

Yes, cbindgen is extremely useful here because it automatically generates the correct header file from your Rust source code using #[repr(C)] structs and extern "C" functions. However, if there are any discrepancies in field names or types between your C header and Rust implementation, you will encounter a mismatch error at compile time when trying to link them together.
Okay, so the mismatch happens during linking? Can I just ignore that error since performance is critical for my game engine? Or should I fix it even if it adds boilerplate code?

You cannot simply ignore that error because a type mismatch will cause the compiler to reject your build entirely. Even though adding boilerplate seems tedious, fixing the ABI alignment ensures predictable memory access patterns which is vital for performance-critical paths in your game engine.
I get it now. But what about the actual calling convention? Does Rust use the standard C calling convention automatically or do I need to specify something extra like std::arch::c_void_pointer?

Rust uses the standard C calling convention automatically when you define an extern "C" function. However, if you are manually creating pointers or performing pointer arithmetic across boundaries, you should use types like *mut c_int or *const c_char to ensure the correct representation is used.
Thanks for breaking that down. One last thing - what happens if I try to pass a Rust owned struct directly into a C function declared with extern "C"? Does it automatically dereference?

No, Rust does not automatically dereference owned structs when passing them to C functions because the memory layout would be invalid for C. You must explicitly create a #[repr(C)] copy of the data or use an FFI-safe wrapper type like c_void or a manually laid-out struct to ensure compatibility.
That makes sense. So the key takeaway is that every interaction with C requires explicit control over layout and linking?

Exactly, every interaction with C requires explicit control over layout and linking. This discipline prevents silent memory corruption bugs that are common when mixing unmanaged languages without strict ABI guarantees.
I think I have a solid understanding now. Thanks for walking me through the extern "C" blocks and link attributes step by step.
You're welcome! Remember to test your C bindings thoroughly before integrating them into production code to avoid subtle runtime failures.

I've been reading about extern blocks and linking attributes but I still can't get my C library to call back into my Rust code correctly. I thought if I just exposed a function in Rust it would automatically work like other languages.

That is a common misconception; Rust does not auto-expose functions for C linkage the way some other languages do, and you must explicitly use extern blocks and link attributes to define the ABI. Think of it like building a bridge: the Rust side builds the piers but you still need to lay down the specific planks (extern blocks) that match exactly what the C side expects before cars can cross over safely. If you skip laying those planks correctly, the traffic will crash at the edge.

Hey, I'm stuck on a production issue. We need to call some legacy C libraries from our new Rust tooling, but I keep hitting strange ABI errors even though I've read about extern "C" blocks and link attributes multiple times.

That's frustrating when the docs say you should be fine. The core issue is usually that Rust types have padding or alignment that C doesn't expect, so the binary representation changes. Think of it like this: if you pack a suitcase with loose change and then ask someone else to carry it without knowing about the loose change, they'll drop some coins because their grip isn't calibrated for the weight distribution you assumed.

Exactly, I've been using standard Rust structs for my data structures and assuming C will just read them. But when I try to call those functions from a C program or vice versa, the memory layout is wrong.

You need to explicitly tell Rust that these types must match C's layout using #[repr(C)] on each struct. Without this attribute, Rust might add padding fields for alignment, which breaks the binary interface. It's like insisting your custom-built box has exactly the same dimensions as a standard shipping container without measuring it first.
Okay, so I should wrap my structs with #[repr(C)] but what about the linking? I've seen people mention link attributes in Cargo.toml or build scripts. Do I need to add something specific there?

Yes, you need to ensure the linker knows how to bind your symbols correctly. In Cargo.toml for Rust projects using cdylib, you specify [[target.'cfg(target_arch=\"x86_64\")\.dependencies]] with link = "static" or similar flags depending on your build system configuration.
I think I understand the linking part now, but what about when C calls Rust? If my Rust code exports functions via extern "C", does C automatically know how to link those symbols without extra flags?

Not necessarily. When C links against a Rust shared library, the dynamic linker needs explicit guidance on which symbols are exported and their ABI stability. You may need to use cbindgen to generate a consistent header file that documents your extern "C" signatures so both sides agree on the interface contract.
Right, I tried running cbindgen but it still produced headers with Rust-specific syntax like use std::os::raw::c_int which C compilers don't like. What should those lines look like instead?

cbindgen should strip out all non-FFI relevant parts and output pure C declarations. If you're still seeing Rust syntax, check that your #[no_mangle] attributes are present on every extern "C" function to prevent symbol mangling in the final binary.
Wait, what's no_mangle? I thought C symbols were automatically named correctly without any Rust annotations?

No, by default Rust mangles function names using the rustc demangler convention which includes type information. For FFI you must use #[no_mangle] to ensure the symbol name remains exactly as written in your extern "C" signature so C can resolve it correctly.
Got it, so every function exported for FFI needs both extern "C" and #[no_mangle]. But what about structs? Do they also need special handling beyond #[repr(C)]?

Structs primarily need #[repr(C)] but if you're generating headers with cbindgen, ensure those structs are marked as #[derive(Debug)] or similar traits only for Rust internals, not included in the C output. The goal is a clean C header that matches your extern "C" functions exactly.

I'm applying these fixes now. One last thing though, if I have multiple structs and want to group them efficiently in the generated header, can I use something like a union or enum to reduce redundancy?

You can absolutely use enums with #[repr(C)] tagged unions to represent different data states without duplicating field definitions. This keeps your C interface lean and easier to maintain across language boundaries.
That makes sense. So my final plan is: wrap structs with #[repr(C)], add #[no_mangle] to functions, use cbindgen for headers, and configure link attributes in Cargo.toml?
Exactly. That combination ensures your Rust types match C's expectations while keeping the symbol interface stable across rebuilds.
Wait, I just realized something. If my Rust library is a staticlib instead of a cdylib, will that still work with C programs without any special linker flags?

Static libraries generally don't require dynamic linking but still need proper symbol visibility control via #[no_mangle] and correct layout via #[repr(C)]. However, if you're embedding Rust code directly in C rather than linking separately, the rules shift slightly toward pure C compilation with inline assembly.

Ah, I think I finally get it. The key insight was that without #[repr(C)] and #[no_mangle], Rust's default type system creates a barrier between C and Rust memory layouts. It's like trying to fit a square peg in a round hole without knowing the hole's dimensions first.
Yes, that analogy captures it perfectly. Now you should be able to compile your FFI interface cleanly with the correct attributes applied.
